remedies: Rest as much as possible for the first 2 to 3 days
Drink at least 8 glasses of water, herbal tea, or clear broth daily
Gargle with warm salt water to soothe a sore throat
Use saline nasal drops or spray to relieve congestion
Take acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever or body aches — follow dosage instructions
Use a humidifier in your room to add moisture to the air
Avoid alcohol and caffeine — they cause dehydration
Honey mixed in warm water or tea can help soothe a cough
watch_for: Fever above 103F (39.4C) — seek medical care
Symptoms lasting more than 10 days without improvement
Symptoms that improve then suddenly worsen
Shortness of breath or difficulty breathing
Severe sinus pain or pain around eyes

remedies: Stay home and rest — avoid contact with others to prevent spreading
Drink plenty of fluids: water, broth, sports drinks to prevent dehydration
Take acetaminophen or ibuprofen to reduce fever and relieve body aches
Do NOT give aspirin to children or teenagers with flu
Use a humidifier to ease congestion and sore throat
Eat light easy-to-digest foods: soup, toast, crackers, bananas
Stay in bed during the worst of the illness — your body needs energy to fight the virus
See a doctor within 48 hours if you are high risk — antiviral medication (Tamiflu) works best when started early
watch_for: Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath — seek emergency care
Persistent chest pain or pressure
Sudden dizziness or confusion
Severe or persistent vomiting
Symptoms improve then return with fever and worse cough
Bluish lips or face — call 911 immediately
flu.duration: 5 to 7 days, fatigue may last 2 weeks

remedies: Take acetaminophen (Tylenol) or ibuprofen (Advil) as directed on the package
Drink plenty of cool fluids — water, juice, broth — to prevent dehydration
Rest and avoid strenuous activity
Wear light comfortable clothing and use a light blanket
Apply a cool damp washcloth to your forehead, wrists, or back of neck
Keep room temperature comfortable — not too hot or too cold
Take a lukewarm bath or sponge bath — avoid cold water which can cause shivering
Do NOT bundle up in heavy blankets if you have chills — this traps heat
watch_for: Fever above 103F (39.4C) in adults — call your doctor
Fever in any infant under 3 months — seek immediate care
Fever with stiff neck and severe headache — possible meningitis, call 911
Fever with difficulty breathing or chest pain — call 911
Fever lasting more than 3 days
Fever with confusion, seizures, or loss of consciousness — call 911
Fever with severe rash
fever.duration: Typically 3 to 5 days depending on cause

remedies: Take over the counter pain relief: acetaminophen, ibuprofen, or aspirin (not for children)
Rest in a quiet dark room — light and noise can worsen headaches
Apply a cold ice pack or warm compress to your head or neck — try both and use whichever helps
Drink a full glass of water — dehydration is a very common headache trigger
Massage your temples, neck, and shoulders gently
Try to sleep — many headaches resolve with rest
Avoid screens, bright lights, and loud sounds
For tension headaches: gentle neck stretches can relieve muscle tightness
Avoid caffeine if prone to headaches — but if you regularly drink coffee, withdrawal can cause headaches
watch_for: Sudden severe headache described as the worst headache of your life — call 911 immediately, possible brain bleed
Headache with fever and stiff neck — possible meningitis, call 911
Headache with vision changes, confusion, or weakness on one side
Headache after a head injury
Headache that gets progressively worse over days
Headache with vomiting that is not related to nausea
Headache in someone over 50 that is new or different from usual
headache.duration: 30 minutes to several hours for tension headache

remedies: Gargle with warm salt water (half teaspoon of salt in 8 ounces of warm water) several times a day
Drink warm liquids: herbal tea with honey, warm broth, or warm water with lemon
Suck on ice chips, popsicles, or throat lozenges to numb the pain
Take acetaminophen or ibuprofen for pain and fever
Use a humidifier to keep air moist — dry air worsens sore throat
Rest your voice and avoid talking loudly
Avoid irritants: cigarette smoke, strong chemicals, very dry air
Stay well hydrated throughout the day
watch_for: Severe sore throat with high fever above 101F and no cough — possible strep throat, needs antibiotics
White patches or pus on tonsils — see a doctor
Difficulty swallowing or breathing — seek emergency care
Drooling because you cannot swallow your saliva — go to ER
Rash along with sore throat — possible scarlet fever
Sore throat lasting more than a week without improvement
Swollen lymph nodes in the neck that are very tender
sore_throat.duration: 5 to 7 days for viral sore throat

remedies: Stop eating solid food until vomiting has completely stopped
Take small sips of clear liquids every 5 to 10 minutes: water, clear broth, ginger ale, electrolyte drinks
Suck on ice chips if you cannot keep liquids down
Gradually introduce bland foods once vomiting stops: crackers, toast, rice, bananas, applesauce
Avoid dairy products, greasy or fatty foods, spicy foods, alcohol, and caffeine
Rest with your head elevated — lying flat can worsen nausea
Try ginger: ginger tea, ginger ale, or ginger candies can help settle the stomach
Fresh air and a cool environment can help reduce nausea
Over the counter antiemetics like Dramamine may help with motion sickness related nausea
watch_for: Blood in vomit — looks red or like dark coffee grounds — call 911
Vomiting combined with severe chest pain — possible heart attack, call 911
Vomiting combined with severe headache and stiff neck — possible meningitis, call 911
Signs of severe dehydration: no urination for 8 hours, extreme dry mouth, dizziness
Cannot keep any liquids down for more than 12 hours
High fever above 103F with vomiting
Vomiting after a head injury
nausea.duration: 24 to 48 hours for most cases of stomach flu

remedies: Eat smaller meals more frequently instead of large meals
Eat slowly and chew food thoroughly
Avoid trigger foods: spicy foods, fatty or greasy foods, chocolate, citrus, tomatoes, onions, garlic
Avoid alcohol and caffeine — both increase stomach acid
Do not lie down for at least 2 to 3 hours after eating
Elevate the head of your bed by 6 to 8 inches if you get nighttime heartburn
Take over the counter antacids (Tums, Rolaids) for immediate relief
Wear loose comfortable clothing — tight clothing puts pressure on the stomach
Maintain a healthy weight — excess weight puts pressure on the stomach
watch_for: Chest pain that could be confused with heartburn — if severe or radiates to arm, call 911
Difficulty swallowing
Persistent vomiting
Black or bloody stools — see a doctor immediately
Unexplained weight loss with indigestion
Symptoms lasting more than 2 weeks despite home treatment
Indigestion that wakes you from sleep regularly
indigestion.duration: Usually resolves within a few hours with antacids

remedies: Take over the counter antihistamines: cetirizine (Zyrtec), loratadine (Claritin), or fexofenadine (Allegra)
Use nasal corticosteroid sprays (Flonase, Nasacort) for nasal congestion — most effective allergy treatment
Use saline nasal rinse (neti pot or spray) to flush allergens from nasal passages
Check daily pollen counts and stay indoors when counts are very high
Keep windows closed during high pollen season — use air conditioning instead
Shower and change clothes after spending time outdoors
Wear sunglasses outdoors to protect eyes from pollen
Vacuum frequently using a HEPA filter vacuum
Decongestants (Sudafed) can help with nasal congestion but should not be used more than 3 days
watch_for: Difficulty breathing or wheezing — may indicate allergic asthma, see a doctor
Symptoms that do not improve with antihistamines after 2 weeks
Facial pain or pressure that may indicate a sinus infection
Symptoms that worsen significantly despite treatment
Hives or skin rash developing along with respiratory symptoms
Throat tightening or swelling — this could indicate anaphylaxis, call 911
seasonal_allergies.duration: Varies by season — typically weeks to months

remedies: Apply ice packs wrapped in a cloth to the painful area for 20 minutes several times a day for the first 48 to 72 hours
After 72 hours switch to a heating pad or warm compress to relax tight muscles
Take ibuprofen or acetaminophen as directed to reduce pain and inflammation
Stay as active as possible — complete bed rest is NOT recommended and slows recovery
Try gentle walking for 10 to 20 minutes several times a day
Avoid activities that worsen the pain but do not stop all movement
Sleep on your side with a pillow between your knees to reduce spine pressure
Avoid lifting heavy objects — if you must lift, bend your knees not your back
Gentle stretching of the lower back and hip muscles can help after the first 72 hours
watch_for: Loss of bladder or bowel control — this is a spinal emergency, go to ER immediately
Numbness or tingling in the groin or inner thighs (saddle area)
Back pain with fever — possible spinal infection or kidney infection
Severe pain that does not improve at all after 72 hours of home treatment
Pain that radiates down both legs with weakness
Back pain after a fall or trauma
Upper back pain with chest pain or shortness of breath — possible heart issue, call 911
back_pain.duration: Most acute back pain improves within 4 to 6 weeks

remedies: Sit or lie down immediately when you feel dizzy — do not try to walk around
Do not drive or operate machinery while dizzy
Drink a full glass of water — dizziness is often caused by dehydration
Move slowly when changing positions — stand up gradually from sitting or lying
Rest in a quiet cool dark room until dizziness passes
Avoid caffeine, alcohol, salt, and tobacco which can worsen dizziness
If dizziness is caused by BPPV (room spinning), ask your doctor about the Epley maneuver
Eat small regular meals to prevent low blood sugar related dizziness
Check your medications — dizziness is a common side effect of many drugs
watch_for: Sudden dizziness with facial drooping, arm weakness, or slurred speech — possible stroke, call 911
Dizziness with severe headache and stiff neck — possible meningitis, call 911
Dizziness with chest pain or shortness of breath — possible heart issue, call 911
Dizziness after a head injury
Dizziness with fainting or loss of consciousness
Dizziness with sudden hearing loss in one ear
Dizziness that is new, severe, or lasts more than one hour
dizziness.duration: Mild dizziness usually passes within minutes to hours
